title: Needle-felted nuthatch
subtitle: A compact blue-grey and apricot nuthatch in merino with a bold eye stripe
excerpt: Needle-felt a compact nuthatch in blue-grey and warm apricot merino, capturing the distinctive black eye stripe and the characteristic head-down posture. The finished piece is about 5 cm long.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. The nuthatch is a common target in British bird-felting guild projects; its compact form and bold markings make it a rewarding intermediate subject.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon; the standard starting material for needle felting.

content.text: The nuthatch is known for its head-down posture on bark. Build the piece at a slight angle to capture this.
gives three main colour zones: blue-grey upper parts in
, warm apricot on the underside, and a narrow black eye stripe.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam mat only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

items.name: Merino roving, warm apricot or buff (underside)
Small cork offcut or section of bark (base, optional)

content.text: Roll about 10 g of blue-grey roving into a compact oval, about 5 cm long and 2 cm in diameter. Stab with the size-38 needle, rotating frequently. The body is slightly bulkier at the chest than the tail.

Cover the upper surface of the body and head in blue-grey. Apply warm apricot to the underside, from the throat to the vent. Apply white to the cheek area on each side. The boundary between blue-grey and apricot runs along the flanks; keep it clean with the size-40 needle.
Step 3: Add the eye stripe and beak

The eye stripe is the key marking. Apply a narrow strip of black roving from the base of where the beak will sit, through the eye position, and back along the side of the nape. Keep this to about 2 to 3 mm wide. Roll a short pointed cone of black roving for the beak, about 7 mm long, and stab it firmly onto the face.
Step 4: Make the tail and feet

Attach a short, slightly flattened piece of blue-grey roving at the rear for the tail, about 2 cm long. For the feet, add two tiny dark grey roving pinches to the underside, slightly forward of the body midpoint, to suggest the gripping feet.
Position the finished nuthatch at a head-downward angle on a cork or bark base. Use a few long needle strokes through the feet and into the cork to hold it in position.

items.problem: The eye stripe curves rather than running straight
items.cause: The roving was not thinned sufficiently before application
items.fix: Tease the black roving to a very thin, even strip before laying it on the face. Work from beak to nape in one continuous placement, then stab from the middle outward.

items.problem: The apricot belly reads as white
items.cause: Roving colour too light or layer too thin
items.fix: Build the apricot in two layers. If the roving is too cream-toned, add a small amount of orange or warm brown roving to deepen the shade.
